frameworks.name: hierarchical Bayesian modeling
frameworks.one_line: Multilevel Bayesian inference framework for AI evaluation statistics

frameworks.name: generalized linear models
frameworks.one_line: GLM backbone extended with Bayesian multilevel structure

frameworks.name: Bayesian data analysis
frameworks.one_line: Principled uncertainty quantification via posterior inference

frameworks.name: formal model comparison
frameworks.one_line: Compares competing models for evaluation outcomes

core_question: How can hierarchical Bayesian modeling robustly estimate AI capabilities and quantify uncertainty from limited, nested evaluation data?
key_assumptions: AI evaluation outputs are inherently stochastic
Evaluations have a nested hierarchical structure
Low-data scenarios require principled uncertainty quantification
GLM family is adequate for evaluation outcome modeling
manipulations: Compared HiBayES against conventional statistical methods
Tested in low-data regimes (< 20 data points per evaluation)
key_findings: HiBayES supports robust inferences in both classical Q-A benchmarks and agentic evaluations
Framework performs well in low-data scenarios under 20 data points per evaluation
Provides principled uncertainty quantification on AI capability estimates
Yields robust parameter estimation versus conventional methods
interpretations: Multilevel Bayesian GLMs are well-suited to the nested, costly, stochastic nature of advanced AI evaluations
Principled uncertainty quantification is increasingly important as LLMs and AI systems evolve
